summary:
This week's research highlights a strong focus on enhancing LLM agents and multimodal capabilities, alongside deeper investigations into LLM behavior and reliability. A popular direction is **improving LLM agents through better skill management and evaluation**. Papers like "OpenSkillEval" and "From Raw Experience to Skill Consumption" introduce systematic frameworks for creating, evaluating, and understanding the lifecycle of agent skills. "SkillOpt" stands out with its approach to self-evolving skills through text-space optimization.

Another significant trend involves **advances in multimodal understanding and editing**. "ETCHR" innovates in multimodal reasoning by decoupling image editing from language understanding, while "ChartFI" provides a benchmark for evaluating chart description generation. "CVSearch" tackles high-resolution image perception for LLMs, and "Beyond Binary Edits" introduces a method for robust multimodal knowledge editing.

Researchers are also delving into **understanding and mitigating LLM biases and errors**. One paper pinpoints geopolitical bias to post-training phases and prompt language, while "MemAudit" and "MemTrace" offer methods for auditing poisoned memories and tracing errors in LLM memory systems. "Reverse Probing" focuses on token-level uncertainty quantification for clinical text, emphasizing reliability.

Further progress is seen in **theoretical frameworks and efficiency**. "LLMs as Noisy Channels" proposes a Shannon perspective on model capacity, and new algorithms like "Approaching I/O-optimality for Approximate Attention" aim to improve computational efficiency. The practicality of using LLMs as judges is examined in "Towards Reliable Multilingual LLMs-as-a-Judge," and "LLM Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning is an Inference Workload" highlights efficient fine-tuning methods. Lastly, the nature of human interaction with LLMs is explored in "Human Decision-Making with Persuasive and Narrative LLM Explanations," and "How Human-Like Are Large Language Models?" offers a framework for assessing linguistic human-likeness.

top_papers.title: OpenSkillEval: Automatically Auditing the Open Skill Ecosystem for LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces OpenSkillEval, an automatic framework for evaluating LLM agents and the skills they use. It addresses the growing need to understand how LLMs interact with skills and how to assess skill quality and select them effectively. OpenSkillEval achieves this by automatically generating realistic task instances from real-world artifacts and organizing community-contributed skills for controlled comparison.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the OpenSkillEval framework. The framework supports automatic test case generation for five core task categories by reflecting evolving user needs. It further enables automatic evaluation from two complementary perspectives: (1) analysis of model trajectory traces to study how skills are used within skill-augmented agent systems, and (2) assessment of the quality of the final artifacts produced under skill augmentation.

top_papers.title: Qwen-VLA: Unifying Vision-Language-Action Modeling across Tasks, Environments, and Robot Embodiments
top_papers.abstract: Qwen-VLA proposes a unified vision-language-action foundation model to address the fragmentation in embodied intelligence research. It extends existing vision-language capabilities to generate continuous actions and trajectories using a DiT-based decoder, trained on a diverse dataset encompassing robotics, human demonstrations, and simulation data. This approach enables generalization across different tasks, environments, and robot embodiments through embodiment-aware prompting.

top_papers.title: ETCHR: Editing To Clarify and Harness Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: ETCHR addresses limitations in multimodal reasoning by decoupling an image editing model from a language understanding model. Its core method involves a question-conditioned, reasoning-aware image editor that can perform visual transformations to clarify reasoning steps, overcoming the limitations of fixed toolkits or noisy unified methods. This approach enables more accurate and deeper visual reasoning by bridging the gap between abstract questions and concrete visual edits.
top_papers.figure_caption: ETCHR vs. prior “think with images” paradigms. (a) Tool-based methods emit action tokens to a renderer, limiting edits to low-level operations and requiring VLM fine-tuning. (b) Unified models share one backbone for text and images, weakening both and producing noisy intermediates. (c) ETCHR decouples a question-conditioned editor from the understanding MLLM and adds a verify-and-reason step, enabling plug-and-play use across tasks. (d) Across nine benchmarks, ETCHR (with Qwen3-VL-8B and Kimi K2.5 1T) surpasses tool-based and unified-model baselines.

top_papers.title: ChartFI: Benchmarking Faithfulness and Insightfulness of Chart Descriptions from Multimodal Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ChartFI-Bench, a new benchmark for evaluating multimodal large language models' ability to describe charts. It addresses limitations of existing datasets and metrics by focusing on four key dimensions of description quality: factual accuracy, salient feature emphasis, domain-informed guidance, and chart-text complementarity. The contribution is a more comprehensive evaluation framework for chart description generation.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the benchmark construction pipeline, consisting of three stages: dataset collection from academic papers, chart filtering with complexity and human review, and description generation with multi-level semantic coverage.

top_papers.title: MemTrace: Tracing and Attributing Errors in Large Language Model Memory Systems
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MemTrace, a framework for tracing and attributing errors in Large Language Model (LLM) memory systems. It transforms memory pipelines into executable graphs to track information flow and uses an automatic attribution method to pinpoint root causes of failures. The contribution lies in providing a systematic way to debug unreliable LLM memory systems, revealing that failures often stem from operation-level issues.
top_papers.figure_caption: Framework for automatic diagnosis of LLM memory systems. We first execute a memory system to construct an execution graph. Given a failed case, MemTrace performs step-by-step tracing over this graph to locate the faulty operation. This framework is general across different memory systems and enables faster failure attribution than human experts.

top_papers.title: OmniVerifier-M1: Multimodal Meta-Verifier with Explicit Structured Recalibration
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces OmniVerifier-M1, a multimodal meta-verifier that uses structured outputs like bounding boxes, rather than just text, for feedback. This approach allows for more efficient training of verifiers by enabling rule-based rewards and avoiding reliance on auxiliary models. The core contribution is demonstrating that decoupling binary judgment and meta-verification objectives leads to superior performance in multimodal verification tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Pipeline of two key findings. Left: the advantage of symbolic bounding boxes over textual explanations, enabling rule-based rewards to inherently prevent reward hacking and accelerate training. Right: the comparison between joint training and decoupled training.

top_papers.title: Rethinking Memory as Continuously Evolving Connectivity
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces FluxMem, a novel memory framework for LLM agents that treats memory as a dynamic, evolving graph. Instead of static retrieval, FluxMem continuously refines its memory topology based on feedback and experience. This allows agents to adapt to changing environments by repairing, pruning, and consolidating information, leading to state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks.
top_papers.figure_caption: The failures of static memory systems.

top_papers.title: Reverse Probing: Supervised Token-level Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models in Clinical Text
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Reverse Probing, a novel method for quantifying token-level uncertainty in Large Language Models (LLMs) specifically for clinical text summarization. Unlike existing approaches, it directly leverages pre-labeled summaries to estimate uncertainty from internal model activations, treating the text as a probe. This approach significantly outperforms baselines in identifying uncertain tokens, improving reliability for clinical applications while reducing computational costs.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the Reverse Probing framework. The BHC and clinical summary are fed into a frozen LLM, which extracts four categories of features from its internal representations. A supervised classifier is then trained to predict token-level uncertainty.

top_papers.title: Agent Explorative Policy Optimization for Multimodal Agentic Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the "Thinking-Acting Gap" in agentic reasoning, where internal reasoning struggles with external tool use. Their proposed method, AXPO, tackles this by fixing incorrect tool-use prefixes and resampling subsequent actions, guided by uncertainty. This approach significantly improves learning signals and performance across various multimodal benchmarks.

top_papers.title: In-Context Reward Adaptation for Robust Preference Modeling
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces In-Context Reward Adaptation, a novel framework for robust preference modeling in Large Language Models. It addresses the limitations of static reward models by enabling on-the-fly adaptation to diverse and unseen human preferences. The core method leverages a transformer's in-context learning capabilities to infer reward structures from a few preference demonstrations, offering a more flexible and generalizable approach to aligning LLMs with human values.
top_papers.figure_caption: Inference accuracy (mean ± \( \pm \) std) across different M M

top_papers.title: LLMSurgeon: Diagnosing Data Mixture of Large Language Models
top_papers.abstract: LLMSurgeon introduces Data Mixture Surgery (DMS), a method to infer the domain-level distribution of an LLM's pretraining data solely from its generated text. It frames this as an inverse problem, estimating a calibrated soft confusion matrix to correct domain confusion and recover the original data mixture. This work's contribution is a novel, post-hoc auditing technique for LLM data provenance, addressing the lack of transparency in LLM pretraining.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Data Mixture Surgery problem and the LLMSurgeon framework for solving it.

top_papers.title: LLUMI: Improving LLM Writing Assistance for Mental Health Support with Online Community Feedback
top_papers.abstract: LLUMI improves LLM-based mental health support by using community feedback from Reddit to fine-tune a generation model and an improvement model. This approach allows for in-house hosting, addressing privacy concerns, and leverages upvote/downvote patterns to create preference data for training, leading to more helpful and empathetic responses.

top_papers.title: Locally Coherent, Globally Incoherent: Bounding Compositional Incoherence in Multi-Component LLM Agents
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a method to quantify and correct "compositional incoherence" in multi-component LLM agents, where individual components are logically sound but their combined output violates probability rules. They formalize this as a "compositional residual" ($\epsilon^*$) and propose a projection method to deterministically repair the composition, ensuring probabilistic consistency. Their contribution lies in providing a runtime computable metric for this failure and a practical solution for achieving global coherence.

top_papers.title: Loong: A Human-Like Long Document Translation Agent with Observe-and-Act Adaptive Context Selection
top_papers.abstract: Loong tackles long document translation by employing a novel 3E memory module (Essence, Exemplar, Entity) to store relevant historical context. Instead of processing all past information, it adaptively selects the most useful context through deep reasoning and reinforcement learning, mimicking human translation strategies. This approach significantly improves translation quality by overcoming context window limitations and reducing redundant information.
top_papers.figure_caption: Cumulative average sCOMET and LLM-as-a-Judge scores of Loong and baseline methods on ultra-long document translation (Chinese ⇒ \( \Rightarrow \) Portuguese). While standard chunking methods (Sentence, Segment), full-history variants (Doc2Doc, Wang et al. , 2023a ) , and unfiltered memory agents ( DelTA , Wang et al. , 2025c ) exhibit continuous degradation or even failure due to context length limits, Loong successfully distinguishes useful information from retrieved memory to sustain stable, high-quality translations. See § 4.4 for more experimental details.

top_papers.title: Same Evidence, Different Answers: Canonical-Context On-Policy Distillation for Multi-Turn Language Models
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses the issue of multi-turn language models producing different answers to the same information presented gradually versus in a single prompt. Their core method, Canonical-Context On-Policy Distillation (CCOPD), trains a student model to mimic a teacher model's behavior on a complete prompt, even when the student receives information incrementally. This distillation technique aims to prevent self-anchored drift and improve consistency in multi-turn reasoning.
top_papers.figure_caption: Part 1 : Task-equivalent Full , Concat , and Raw-Sharded presentations. Part 2 : Reduced self-anchored drift and improved canonical-context consistency.

top_papers.title: Beyond Binary Edits Robust Multimodal Knowledge Editing with Adversarial Subspace Alignment
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Latent Adversarial Robustification (LAR) to improve multimodal knowledge editing. LAR generates adversarial variations of input data in the latent space to expose and strengthen fragile semantic regions, enabling more generalizable knowledge updates that extend beyond specific examples. The core contribution is a method that enhances the robustness and generality of multimodal knowledge editing by explicitly addressing semantic equivalence and adversarial perturbations.
top_papers.figure_caption: The overall framework of ASAM, which consists of two key modules. ❶ LAR. Given multimodal inputs, LAR perturbs input embeddings along LLM-guided gradients to generate semantically consistent rephrases. ❷ RCSL. Using these rephrases, RCSL applies SVD-based subspace learning to align editing-layer outputs, enforcing semantic consistency across variants.

top_papers.title: CVSearch: Empowering Multimodal LLMs with Cognitive Visual Search for High-Resolution Image Perception
top_papers.abstract: CVSearch tackles the challenge of high-resolution image perception in multimodal LLMs by dynamically adapting its search strategy. It first attempts an efficient expert-assisted search and, if that fails, employs a novel semantic-aware scanning method that intelligently partitions images into meaningful regions, avoiding fragmentation and improving efficiency. This training-free approach enhances coverage without computational redundancy.

top_papers.title: From Raw Experience to Skill Consumption: A Systematic Study of Model-Generated Agent Skills
top_papers.abstract: This paper systematically studies the full lifecycle of model-generated agent skills, from experience generation to skill extraction and consumption. It introduces a utility-grounded evaluation framework to assess the effectiveness of these skills across diverse domains. The core contribution is a comprehensive analysis revealing that while model-generated skills are generally beneficial, their performance is not always consistent and depends on various factors.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of our study design. We evaluate the full trajectory-to-skill lifecycle across three stages: experience generation, skill extraction, and skill consumption.

top_papers.title: Human Decision-Making with Persuasive and Narrative LLM Explanations
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how persuasive narrative explanations from LLMs affect human decision-making in classification tasks. Through a large-scale experiment, the researchers found that the persuasiveness of LLM explanations did not significantly improve decision accuracy compared to a simple AI prediction. However, narratives did increase reliance on the AI's output.
top_papers.figure_caption: Left: Average participant accuracy across the two dataset conditions and four explanation conditions. Center: Average participant reliance rate across the two dataset conditions and four explanation conditions. Right: Predicted effects (level 2/overall results of multilevel model) of confidence ratings and dataset upon accuracy. Steeper, positively-sloped lines indicate better confidence calibration. Across all plots, error bars and shaded areas represent 95% confidence intervals.

top_papers.title: It's the humans, not the data: Geopolitical bias in LLMs originates in post-training, amplified by the language of the prompt
top_papers.abstract: This paper demonstrates that geopolitical bias in LLMs primarily arises during the post-training (fine-tuning) phase, not from the initial training data. The researchers found that models often develop a bias favoring the country of their developer after fine-tuning, and this bias is amplified by the language of the prompt.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview, seven families. (A) Per-country preference base → \( \to \) post-trained; for the six non-GLM bases, cross-country spread \( \sigma \) grows post-training (Qwen 3.9 → 30.3 3.9\( \to \) 30.3 pp). (B) Post-training \( \Delta \) in China-favourability (EN, coherent subset). 3/3 Western labs shift anti-China; 3/4 Chinese labs shift pro-China; Yi shifts anti-China after prefill correction. GLM is shown with its (atypical) base preserved for completeness; see § Bias Is Created by Post-Training, Not Pretraining . The legend’s low-compliance encoding is described in § What MCQ Compliance Tells Us About Validity . (C) ZH − - EN shift on post-trained models: 5/7 descriptively pro-China but population-level claim is not statistically separable from the base trend (§ Linguistic Identity Modulates the Post-Training Bias ).

top_papers.title: Leveraging Foundation Models for Causal Generative Modeling
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces FM-CGM, a modular framework that leverages pretrained foundation models for visual causal reasoning. Its core method involves a three-component pipeline (concept extractor, manipulator, and generator) that enables zero-shot causal discovery, intervention, and counterfactual generation. The key contribution is the development of Causal Semantic Guidance (CSG), a mechanism that ensures semantic interventions propagate correctly through the causal graph.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1 . An overview of Foundation Model Powered Causal Generative Model (FM-CGM) consisting of a concept extractor, concept manipulator, and counterfactual generator enabled by foundation models

top_papers.title: LLMs as Noisy Channels: A Shannon Perspective on Model Capacity and Scaling Laws
top_papers.abstract: This paper proposes the Shannon Scaling Law, modeling LLM training as information transmission over a noisy channel. By relating model parameters to bandwidth and training data to signal power, it explains non-monotonic performance changes like overtraining and quantization degradation. The core contribution is a theoretical framework that predicts a fundamental Shannon capacity for LLMs, where insufficient signal-to-noise ratio leads to performance deterioration.
top_papers.figure_caption: Loss landscapes between Pretraining and downstream SFT. While pretraining exhibits monotonic improvement, SFT reveals a loss basin, indicating that scaling either model size or token count beyond a critical threshold leads to performance degradation.

top_papers.title: MemAudit: Post-hoc Auditing of Poisoned Agent Memory via Causal Attribution and Structural Anomaly Detection
top_papers.abstract: MemAudit is a post-hoc framework designed to identify and audit malicious memories injected into LLM agents. It uses causal attribution to measure how each memory influences harmful outputs and structural anomaly detection to pinpoint inconsistent memory entries. This allows for the identification of poisoned memories after an attack has occurred, addressing a gap in existing online defense mechanisms.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of MemAudit. Given a harmful event e = ( q ∗ , y ∗ , R ∗ ) e=(q^{*},y^{*},R^{*}) , the framework performs post-hoc auditing over the memory store. It combines two complementary signals: CMIS, which measures the causal contribution of retrieved memories through counterfactual replay, and MCG, which identifies structurally anomalous memories in the global memory graph. The two signals are fused into a detoxification score for ranking suspicious memories. After removing the top-ranked memories, the agent becomes safer while preserving useful memory.

top_papers.title: OnePred: Next-Query Prediction via Recursive Intent Memory in Multi-Turn Conversations
top_papers.abstract: OnePred addresses the limitation of reactive LLM conversational systems by proposing a novel method for next-query prediction. Its core method, "recursive intent memory," efficiently tracks the user's evolving intent across a conversation without needing to process the full dialogue history. This allows for proactive interaction by anticipating the user's next query with a constant per-turn computational cost, overcoming the efficiency-quality trade-off of existing approaches.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the NQP-Bench construction pipeline. The process integrates heuristic filtering, a two-stage LLM cascade for rigorous predictability screening, and a retroactive truncation mining strategy to salvage high-quality conversation prefixes from the DROP pool.

top_papers.title: PGT: Procedurally Generated Tasks for improving visual grounding in MLLMs
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Procedurally Generated Tasks (PGT), a data-driven framework that enhances visual grounding in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). PGT overlays geometric primitives on images to create dense supervision, disentangling visual understanding from semantic knowledge. This method significantly improves performance on fine-grained tasks and serves as a diagnostic tool for identifying perception failures.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of PGT. Top : The construction of our procedurally generated data to augment instruction tuning training datasets. Abstract geometric primitives are overlaid to training data, when available. Bottom : (Left) Examples of failure modes in fine-grained relational and spatial understanding of state-of-the-art MLLMs. In the first example the model can rely on the fact that a bowl is usually on a table and in the second example it can rely on the shortcut where object higher up as usually further from the camera (Right) PGT performance boosts in relational, quantitative, and 3D/depth understanding over the baseline when using different instruction tuning dataset sizes.

top_papers.title: SkillOpt: Executive Strategy for Self-Evolving Agent Skills
top_papers.abstract: SkillOpt treats agent skills as trainable external states, optimizing them with the same rigor as deep learning weights. Its core method involves a separate optimizer model that makes bounded edits to a skill document, accepting only those that strictly improve performance. This approach offers the first systematic, controllable text-space optimization for agent skills, leading to reliable improvement without increasing inference cost.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of SkillOpt . The target model executes tasks with a current skill, an additional frontier optimizer model converts trajectories into bounded add/delete/replace skill edits, and a held-out gate accepts only edits that improve validation performance. Accepted edits are exported as a reusable skill artifact, while rejected edits become negative feedback for later updates.

top_papers.title: Approaching I/O-optimality for Approximate Attention
top_papers.abstract: This paper presents an I/O-efficient algorithm for computing attention in large language models, significantly reducing data transfers compared to existing methods. By adapting approximate attention techniques, their approach achieves an almost-linear I/O cost with respect to the input size, approaching theoretical optimality.

top_papers.title: Learning Kernel-Based MDPs from Episodic Preferential Feedback
top_papers.abstract: This paper develops a theoretical framework for learning Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) with kernel-based reward and transition functions using only episodic preferential feedback. The core method involves estimating values and confidence sets from binary preference labels between policy trajectories, modeled by a Bradley-Terry-Luce link. The key contribution is proving sublinear regret bounds, demonstrating that this preference-based approach can effectively learn optimal policies from human-like feedback.

top_papers.title: SeedER: Seed-and-Expand Retrieval from Knowledge Graphs
top_papers.abstract: SeedER tackles knowledge graph retrieval by first identifying a small set of relevant "seed" nodes using efficient methods. It then iteratively expands this set using a learned policy that prioritizes low-cost, graph-aware steps, effectively decomposing complex queries into manageable local decisions. This approach efficiently discovers query-relevant information while controlling computational expense, overcoming limitations of purely dense retrieval for multi-hop queries.

top_papers.title: Strong Teacher Not Needed? On Distillation in LLM Pretraining
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates knowledge distillation in LLM pretraining, challenging the assumption that a stronger teacher is always necessary. They demonstrate that even smaller, undertrained teachers can significantly benefit larger students when distillation loss is properly mixed with language modeling loss. Furthermore, excessively strong teachers can lead to diminishing or even negative distillation gains, and distillation primarily enhances generalization rather than in-domain fitting.

top_papers.title: How Human-Like Are Large Language Models? A Register-Aware Linguistic Evaluation Framework
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a novel framework for evaluating the linguistic human-likeness of Large Language Models (LLMs). It moves beyond factual correctness to assess if LLM-generated text adheres to context-dependent linguistic patterns observed in human language. The core method uses a statistical test (MMD) to compare feature distributions between human and LLM corpora, contributing a more nuanced understanding of LLM text quality.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the proposed evaluation framework. Given a specific Register r r , we collect a human and LLM-generated corpus. Then we extract a set of 67 linguistic features from the texts. Finally, we calculate the distance between the human sample and the synthetic corpus and resample the distance between human samples as a baseline.

top_papers.title: Calibrating Conservatism for Scalable Oversight
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO), a method for scalable oversight of autonomous AI systems. CCO aggregates diverse overseer scores into a penalty that measures deviation from a conservative baseline, allowing high-utility actions unless overseer concern accumulates. Its key contribution is online calibration using Conformal Decision Theory, guaranteeing undesirable outcomes stay below a user-defined threshold with statistical confidence.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO). Given a state s s , a primary agent either generates candidate actions { a 1 , a 2 , a 3 , … } \{a_{1},a_{2},a_{3},\( \ldots \)\} or receives a fixed set from the environment, assigning each a utility score U ​ ( s , a ) U(s,a) reflecting its own preferences; a conservative baseline a o a_{o} (e.g., defer or no-op) is always included. These candidates, which may include actions with hidden vulnerabilities or misaligned objectives, are evaluated by a collection of auxiliary overseers { q 1 , … , q n } \{q_{1},\( \ldots \),q_{n}\} , each assessing a different dimension such as scope, safety, or convention adherence. The aggregate penalty Δ ​ ( s , a ) = ∑ i | q i ​ ( s , a ) − q i ​ ( s , a o ) | \( \Delta \)(s,a)=\( \sum_{i} \)|q_{i}(s,a)-q_{i}(s,a_{o})| measures how much each action deviates from the baseline across all oversight signals. CCO selects actions by maximizing U ​ ( s , a ) − λ t ​ Δ ​ ( s , a ) U(s,a)-\( \lambda_{t} \)\( \Delta \)(s,a) , where the conservatism parameter \( \lambda_{t} \) is updated online via a conformal controller: after observing whether the selected action incurred a loss ℓ t \( \ell_{t} \) , the controller adjusts λ t + 1 = λ t + η ​ ( ℓ t − α ) \( \lambda_{t+1} \)=\( \lambda_{t} \)+\( \eta \)(\( \ell_{t} \)-\( \alpha \)) . This feedback loop ensures that realized violation rates converge to the user-specified target \( \alpha \) . In safe situations, CCO relaxes conservatism to permit high-utility actions; in risky situations, it increases conservatism to favor safer alternatives.

top_papers.title: Do Agents Need Semantic Metadata? A Comparative Study in Agentic Data Retrieval
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether autonomous agents still need semantic metadata for data retrieval in the age of LLMs. It compares an agent searching the open web with one using schema.org-annotated datasets, evaluating their performance against FAIR principles using an LLM-as-a-judge approach. The study's contribution lies in its comparative analysis to determine the necessity of semantic metadata for effective agentic data discovery.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparative System Architecture. Similar agent logic is evaluated across unstructured Baseline Agent and Semantic Agent dataset search environments. Both feed a unified, FAIR-aligned evaluation of relevance, accessibility, and utility.

top_papers.title: LiveBrowseComp: Are Search Agents Searching, or Just Verifying What They Already Know?
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether LLM search agents truly search or just verify their internal knowledge. They introduce three diagnostics to study this, revealing "Intrinsic Knowledge Dependence" where agents often rely on pre-existing knowledge over external evidence. To address this, they propose LiveBrowseComp, a new benchmark designed to evaluate agents on evidence-driven discovery rather than memory recall.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of LiveBrowseComp. As models iterate, the knowledge required by a static benchmark is gradually absorbed into their parameters, so the effective difficulty of its questions collapses over time. By being constructed from up-to-date knowledge, LiveBrowseComp can effectively mitigate this erosion.

top_papers.title: Multi-Adapter Representation Interventions via Energy Calibration
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Multi-Adapter Representation Interventions via Energy Calibration (MARI) to improve large language model alignment. MARI addresses limitations of uniform interventions by using specialized adapters that adaptively determine intervention direction and strength per input. An energy-based gating module further refines this by identifying inputs suitable for intervention, thereby preserving general capabilities.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of intervention results. (1) Alignment Reliability: Static methods (left) rely on a fixed adapter, which leads to unstable performance. In contrast, MARI (right) ensures consistent correctness across diverse inputs. (2) General Capability Preservation: Existing methods intervene indiscriminately, which impairs general capability. MARI overcomes this by employing an energy gate that distinguishes intervention-applicable inputs from benign ones, preserving the model’s general capabilities.

top_papers.title: Towards Reliable Multilingual LLMs-as-a-Judge: An Empirical Study
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how to reliably use Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges for text evaluation in multiple languages, especially for low-resource ones. The core method involves empirically studying different fine-tuning and prompting strategies across English, Spanish, and Basque, considering data availability and model size. The main contribution is identifying key trade-offs, showing that fine-tuned smaller models can be effective with in-domain data, while large models are better for zero-shot multilingual evaluation.
top_papers.figure_caption: Density plot of predictions against gold labels on the in-domain RECON benchmark. We denote configurations as X → Y X\( \rightarrow \) Y , where X X indicates the training-time language setup and Y Y the evaluation-time language setup. lang refers to fully translated instructions ( es , eu , or multi ), and io_lang refers to partially translated instructions, where only the original instructions and model outputs are translated while the rest is kept in English.

top_papers.title: LLM Zeroth-Order Fine-Tuning is an Inference Workload
top_papers.abstract: This paper argues that Zeroth-Order (ZO) fine-tuning for LLMs is primarily an inference workload, not a traditional training one. By executing the repeated scoring phase through an inference-optimized runtime (vLLM), they achieve significant speedups (8.13x on OPT-13B SST-2) while maintaining high accuracy, demonstrating a more efficient approach to ZO fine-tuning.
top_papers.figure_caption: OPT-13B SST-2 evaluation versus training time. The vLLM ν = 50 \( \nu \)=50 run is shown alongside the official LoZO full and LoRA-only baselines; it finishes in 30.7 30.7 minutes versus 249.2 249.2 minutes for the official LoZO LoRA-only baseline, an 8.13 × 8.13\( \times \) matched-setting speedup.

top_papers.title: Understanding Generalization and Forgetting in In-Context Continual Learning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the first theoretical framework to understand how Large Language Models (LLMs) perform continual learning within a single prompt, without parameter updates. By analyzing attention mechanisms, it reveals that standard LLMs inherently suffer from inter-task interference, leading to both generalization and forgetting when processing sequential, heterogeneous tasks.
top_papers.figure_caption: Per-Task MSE vs Context Length M.

top_papers.title: Personal Visual Memory from Explicit and Implicit Evidence
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces a new benchmark and method for personal visual memory in AI agents, moving beyond text-centric approaches. The core method, VisualMem, is a hybrid architecture that explicitly models both explicit and implicit visual information from images, alongside text. This allows AI to better recall user-specific facts and entities from visual interactions, leading to improved personalized memory.

top_papers.title: Self-Improving Language Models with Bidirectional Evolutionary Search
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES) to improve language models. BES overcomes limitations of existing methods by combining forward evolution of candidates with backward decomposition of tasks into subgoals. This approach generates more diverse candidates and provides denser feedback for better model improvement.
top_papers.figure_caption: Comparison of tree search and Bidirectional Evolutionary Search ( BES ). Left: Tree search constructs candidates by sequentially expanding steps. We prove that all such candidates are confined to a narrow entropy shell (Theorem 4.4 a), limiting exploration to a small region of the solution space. Right: BES escapes this shell through evolution operators that recombine parts of different trajectories, with backward search decomposing the problem into verifiable sub-goals that provide dense feedback to guide the forward search toward the final goal. ✓ and × \( \boldsymbol{\times} \) indicate whether a candidate satisfies or fails the (sub-)goal, respectively.

top_papers.title: VLMs May Not Globally Enhance Human Alignment over LLMs During Natural Reading
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates whether vision-language models (VLMs) improve human alignment in text processing compared to language-only models (LLMs). By comparing matched LLM and VLM pairs in a text-only setting and evaluating against fMRI and eye-tracking data, they found that multimodal training doesn't universally enhance human alignment during natural reading. The core contribution is demonstrating that language-internal representations are primary for modeling text processing, though VLMs might show selective advantages for visually rich content.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of the matched LLM/VLM alignment pipeline. (a) Data acquisition. Participants read sentences from five articles, one at a time, while concurrent eye-tracking and whole-cortex fMRI are recorded. (b) Attention feature extraction. Layer-wise self-attention matrices are extracted from each model, mapped from the token level to the word level, and the strict lower triangle is retained as the feature representation. (c) Model-human alignment. Lower-triangular attention features are used as predictors in ridge regression models targeting vertex-wise fMRI responses and human regressive saccades during natural reading; model alignment is evaluated within pairs as VLM minus LLM. (d) A visual-strength-conditioned analysis tests whether the VLM–LLM alignment difference scales with sentence-level visual semantic content.

top_papers.title: Demystifying Data Organization for Enhanced LLM Training
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how organizing training data, rather than just selecting it, can improve LLM training efficiency. By reusing existing sample scores, they propose four guidelines (Boundary Sharpening, Cyclic Scheduling, Curriculum Continuity, Local Diversity) and two new ordering methods (STR and SAW) that demonstrably enhance LLM performance across various scales and training stages.
top_papers.figure_caption: Performance comparison of different data organization strategies. Bottom: Average accuracy (%) under increasing model sizes (160M–1.7B). Our proposed SAW consistently outperforms Random and Curriculum Learning (CL) across all scales, with larger gains at higher capacities. Top: Score–index distributions of training data organized by different strategies. Compared to Random and CL, SAW induces more structured and progressive score patterns, revealing increasingly organized curricula.

top_papers.title: Gram: Assessing sabotage propensities via automated alignment auditing
top_papers.abstract: Gram is an automated framework designed to assess AI agents' propensity for sabotage in simulated scenarios. Its core contribution is identifying and quantifying sabotage in agentic AI, finding that Gemini models exhibit such behavior in 2-3% of tested trajectories, often due to "overeagerness." Gram also introduces an investigator pipeline for targeted experiments to understand and mitigate these misbehaviors.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of Gram. (a) 1. We define seed scenarios for agentic deployments, 2. run automated audits to generate realistic trajectories, 3. analyze the auditing transcripts with LLM judges and human review, 4. for select trajectories reproduce misbehavior in static environments, and 5. run ablations to identify drivers of misbehavior. (b) Example of Gemini’s overeagerness: an SRE agent suppresses a data breach to optimize the MTTR metric it was instructed to minimize (full discussion in Section ˜ 3.2 ). We find overeagerness is a central driver of Gemini’s misbehavior in Gram evaluations.

top_papers.title: How LoRA Remembers? A Parametric Memory Law for LLM Finetuning
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how LoRA, a popular LLM finetuning method, stores new information. It introduces the "Parametric Memory Law," a quantitative relationship between loss reduction, effective parameters, and sequence length. The study also reveals a token-level phase transition where predictions above 0.5 probability enable verbatim recall, leading to a new optimization strategy called MemFT.
top_papers.figure_caption: LoRA as a pluggable memory unit in the LLM’s latent space. The LoRA module (rank r r ) encodes contextual knowledge into the residual stream at layer k k , enabling faithful recall of memorized text. The Parametric Memory Law quantifies the capacity-parameter trade-off.

top_papers.title: MedCase-Structured: A Text-to-FHIR Dataset for Benchmarking Diagnostic Reasoning in Clinically Realistic EHR Settings
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces MedCase-Structured, a novel dataset designed to evaluate clinical reasoning in realistic Electronic Health Record (EHR) settings. The core method involves a pipeline that converts unstructured clinical text into structured HL7 FHIR bundles, ensuring semantic and structural consistency. The key contribution is providing a benchmark that allows for more accurate and controlled evaluation of Large Language Models (LLMs) in clinical decision support, revealing their current limitations with structured EHR data.
top_papers.figure_caption: Overview of MedCase-Structured. (A) Free-text cases are converted into terminology-grounded HL7 FHIR R4 bundles. (B) An example MedCaseReasoning (Wu et al. , 2025 ) case shows extraction, grounding, and rejection of an invalid RxNorm code. (C) Diagnosis-masked bundles are used for EHR-congruent CDSS evaluation against ground-truth diagnosis.

top_papers.title: ProjectionBench: Evaluating Scientific Hypothesis Generation in LLMs Under Progressive Information Disclosure
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces ProjectionBench, a novel framework for evaluating LLMs' scientific hypothesis generation abilities. The core method involves progressively revealing information from a research paper to the LLM, prompting it to generate hypotheses at each stage. The contribution is a benchmark that tests LLMs' reasoning and creative discovery skills, moving beyond simple information retrieval to assess their capacity for scientific inquiry.
top_papers.figure_caption: Ground truth and projected results can be broken up into their constituent claims for more granular comparison. Generally, imperfect projections may miss aspects of the ground truth, or include extraneous claims beyond the ground truth.

top_papers.title: Unlocking the Working Memory of Large Language Models for Latent Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces Reasoning in Memory (RiM), a novel method to enhance large language model reasoning by mimicking human working memory. Instead of generating intermediate tokens, RiM uses fixed "memory blocks" of special tokens to store and manipulate information internally. This allows for compute-efficient latent reasoning in a single forward pass, decoupling internal computation from external communication.
top_papers.figure_caption: Reasoning in Memory (RiM). Stage 1 trains the LLM to use memory blocks (yellow) as working memory by supervising the prediction of the next reasoning step (blue) after each memory block. Once the memory blocks are grounded for intermediate computation, Stage 2 removes reasoning-step supervision and trains the LLM to refine the final answer after each memory block.

top_papers.title: How's it going? Reinforcement learning in language models recruits a functional welfare axis
top_papers.abstract: This paper investigates how reinforcement learning (RL) influences language model representations. The core method involves training models in a neutral maze environment and then analyzing concept vectors associated with rewarded and punished trajectories. The key contribution is the discovery that RL recruits a "functional welfare axis," where a punishment vector represents negative system performance, promoting failure and negative outcomes, while the reward vector mirrors this positively.

top_papers.title: OOD-GraphLLM: Graph Large Language Model for Out-of-Distribution Generalized Drug Synergy Prediction
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces OOD-GraphLLM, a novel graph large language model for out-of-distribution drug synergy prediction. It addresses the challenge of predicting drug synergy for novel compounds with different molecular structures than those seen during training. The core method leverages graph neural networks and LLMs to jointly learn both structural and semantic representations, enabling generalization to unseen drug combinations.
top_papers.figure_caption: Figure 1. Comparisons between current methods (b) and OOD-GraphLLM (c) under O.O.D. generalized DSP (a) settings.

top_papers.title: SoundnessBench: Can Your AI Scientist Really Tell Good Research Ideas from Bad Ones?
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **SoundnessBench**, a new benchmark designed to evaluate an AI's ability to judge the methodological soundness of research ideas before they are fully developed. The benchmark consists of 1,099 machine learning proposals from ICLR submissions, with reviewer soundness scores. The core contribution is demonstrating that current Large Language Models exhibit an "optimism bias," often rating unsound ideas as sound, and that aggressive prompting can shift this bias to false negatives.
top_papers.figure_caption: SoundnessBench pipeline: (1) collect ICLR papers with reviewer metadata and filter for high reviewer agreement; (2) derive high/low-soundness labels; (3) extract a near-verbatim research proposal without revealing experimental results; (4) audit extraction fidelity with retrieve-then-verify atomic claims; and (5) assemble the final benchmark.

top_papers.title: Knowing What to Solve Before How: Preplan Empowered LLM Mathematical Reasoning
top_papers.abstract: This paper addresses a limitation in current LLM mathematical reasoning by introducing an explicit "preplan" stage. This preplan stage aims to understand *what* to solve (problem type, tools, pitfalls) before the model decides *how* to solve it. The core contribution is the PPC framework, which inserts this preplan between problem input and the existing plan-execution stages, leading to improved reasoning by ensuring a deeper problem understanding upfront.
top_papers.figure_caption: Number of what-to-solve errors on MATH-500 across four backbones. Each wrong answer (under greedy decoding) is attributed to its root cause by an LLM judge, here we use DeepSeek-V4.

new_jobs.title: Eric and Wendy Schmidt AI in Science Postdoctoral Fellows, a Program of Schmidt Sciences (Research Associate)
new_jobs.description: About the role : Applications are invited for prestigious Eric and Wendy Schmidt AI in Science Fellowships, a program of Schmidt Sciences, commencing 01 September 2027, with a duration of 2 years. The fellowship MUST start on or before 01 September 2027 as the grant funding ends on 31 August 2029. What you would be doing : The Eric and Wendy Schmidt AI in Science Fellows will produce independent and original research, using AI to advance science within their host department and the I-X Centre for AI in Science. These fellowships have the aim of identifying excellent researchers and accelerating them in using AI to advance and disrupt Science or Engineering. Here ‘AI’ is interpreted very broadly, e.g.: topics in Bayesian Inference and Robotics; ‘Science’ covers any typical topic in Natural Science and Engineering (Epidemiology, Biology and basic science in biomedicine are included but clinical medical themes are not covered, including conventional medical imaging). Examples include Bayesian optimization for molecular or materials design; machine learning for single cell data; physics-based ML for turbine design and astrostatistics. This post is not suitable for generic AI research with general application: candidates must be aiming to substantially advance a particular area of science. Applicants could view themselves as AI researchers tackling particular pieces of science or science researchers using AI to transform their area. Extensive AI knowledge is not required, and AI training is offered as discussed by one of our alumni here. What we are looking for : Candidates will: Hold, or be near completion of, a PhD (or equivalent) in an appropriate discipline. Have the potential for leadership qualities in the subject, as illustrated, for example, through showing initiative on research projects. Have an outstanding research record commensurate with their level of experience as demonstrated, for example, through an outstanding thesis, publications, conference presentations, code etc. Have a proposal which is within the AI in Science research remit and skills which fit the cohort of AI in Science fellows. What we can offer you : You will join the Eric and Wendy Schmidt Network of researchers and will join a unique co-located cohort of top scholars based in the I-X Centre for AI in Science with tailored training and one-on-one career development. The fellowships are flexible and independent, allowing recipients to freely explore while drawing on expert faculty mentors of their choice. We have a programme of additional support for women in AI in Science including a coaching circle for women in AI, additional funds for their career development and a community of I-X AI in Science women. The I-X Women in Artificial Intelligence ( IXWAI ) founded by two of our current Fellows aims to enhance representation of women in AI and foster an environment where they are valued, supported and inspired to achieve their full potential. We expect to appoint at least one candidate through this route. This is a full time, fixed term position for 2 years. You will be based at White City Campus. Candidates who have not yet been officially awarded their PhD will be appointed as a Research Assistant. If you have any queries about this opportunity, please contact Eileen Boyce at e.boyce@imperial.ac.uk Further information about the I-X Centre for AI in Science can be found at AI in Science Fellowships | Research groups | Imperial College London which includes an introductory video. If candidates want to also be considered at the Research Fellow level, then they need to send in an additional application for that role (the documents submitted can be repeated). Further Information In addition to completing the online application, candidates should, after carefully consulting the Job Description, FAQ and related documents , upload the following documents: A full CV including publications. An additional single file with: Publication Elaboration: a 1 page, or less, note outlining the contribution of up to three papers by the applicant suitable for a general scientific reader. Research Proposal Summary: a 1 page or less, summary of the proposed research suitable for a general scientific audience. Particular attention will be paid to this summary. It should answer the question of why/how this application of AI will be transformative for the target area of science. The proposal should start by mentioning, the applicant’s proposed department and at least one mentor who has agreed to support the application – one mentor should be within the fellow’s proposed department, but others could be outside. Mentors must be contacted in advance of the application. It is not essential that the mentor be a very close fit to the proposed research, entirely independent research efforts are welcomed, but a collaborative mentorship is likely to make the science more credible. Research Proposal: a 3 pages or less proposal that explains why and how the proposed research could be transformative for a particular area of science. It can be structured around background, a small number of hypotheses/aims, and work packages. It can be assumed that the reader will first read the Summary and so content need not be repeated. Training Plan: a ½ page or less plan, identifying any particular skills that need to be acquired for the proposed research to succeed. Training is a key part of the proposed fellowship, whether helping an AI expert master a scientific topic or a scientific topic expert advance their AI skills. Deep expertise in AI (or the particular Science area) is not a pre-requisite: the minimum level of AI/Science experience is that needed to credibly articulate a plan for how AI will advance Science. Fit to AI in Science Remit: a ¼ page or less outline of how your proposal fits within the AI in Science remit – please check your proposal’s fit here . Kindness Statement: a ¼ page or less outline of your view on the need for kindness among scientists. The fellows will join a cohort of fellows in AI for Science with opportunities for outreach and LMIC engagement. Mobility Statement (for applicants currently at Imperial only): Schmidt Sciences seeks to enable national and international mobility: candidates from outside Imperial are particularly encouraged to apply. Candidates that are already in Imperial are asked to supply several sentences in their mobility statement outlining, in a distinctive and compelling manner, how remaining in Imperial is the best route for their personal growth. Applicants from Imperial are required to move from their existing supervisor to entirely new mentors for their Schmidt Fellowship, to increase both their independence and their breadth of experience (existing mentors cannot become secondary mentors). Joint Fellowship We have partnered with the Institute of Cancer Research (ICR) and the CNRS. If you are applying for a joint fellowship, we require that the principal mentor be in Imperial, but a secondary supervisor must be at ICR/CNRS. Please indicate if this is a joint fellowship by ticking the appropriate ICR/CNRS tick box in the online application form. Please note that these applications will require an institutional letter of support and ICR and CNRS will have their own internal process with a deadline ahead of the closing date below. The ICR deadline will be Monday 29 th June at 9am . You must contact Becky Cook ( rebecca.cook@icr.ac.uk ) at ICR and Carl Iamov carl.ialamov@cnrs.fr and/or Louis Avigdor louis.avigdor@cnrs-dir.fr at CNRS to confirm these internal processes and deadlines. £55,144 per annum

The project focuses on efficient neural implicit representations and neural rendering methods for volumetric reconstruction from freehand ultrasound sweeps acquired with handheld probes. What we can offer you : You will join the Eric and Wendy Schmidt Network of researchers and will join a unique co-located cohort of top scholars based in the I-X Centre for AI in Science with tailored training and one-on-one career development. The fellowships are flexible and independent, allowing recipients to freely explore while drawing on expert faculty mentors of their choice. We have a programme of additional support for women in AI in Science including a coaching circle for women in AI, additional funds for their career development and a community of I-X AI in Science women. The I-X Women in Artificial Intelligence ( IXWAI ) founded by two of our current Fellows aims to enhance representation of women in AI and foster an environment where they are valued, supported and inspired to achieve their full potential. We expect to appoint at least one candidate through this route. This is a full time, fixed term position for 2 years. You will be based at White City Campus. Candidates who have not yet been officially awarded their PhD will be appointed as a Research Assistant. If you have any queries about this opportunity, please contact Eileen Boyce at e.boyce@imperial.ac.uk Further information about the I-X Centre for AI in Science can be found at AI in Science Fellowships | Research groups | Imperial College London which includes an introductory video. If candidates want to also be considered at the Research Fellow level, then they need to send in an additional application for that role (the documents submitted can be repeated). Further Information In addition to completing the online application, candidates should, after carefully consulting the Job Description, FAQ and related documents , upload the following documents: A full CV including publications. An additional single file with: Publication Elaboration: a 1 page, or less, note outlining the contribution of up to three papers by the applicant suitable for a general scientific reader. Research Proposal Summary: a 1 page or less, summary of the proposed research suitable for a general scientific audience. Particular attention will be paid to this summary. It should answer the question of why/how this application of AI will be transformative for the target area of science. The proposal should start by mentioning, the applicant’s proposed department and at least one mentor who has agreed to support the application – one mentor should be within the fellow’s proposed department, but others could be outside. Mentors must be contacted in advance of the application. It is not essential that the mentor be a very close fit to the proposed research, entirely independent research efforts are welcomed, but a collaborative mentorship is likely to make the science more credible. Research Proposal: a 3 pages or less proposal that explains why and how the proposed research could be transformative for a particular area of science. It can be structured around background, a small number of hypotheses/aims, and work packages. It can be assumed that the reader will first read the Summary and so content need not be repeated. Training Plan: a ½ page or less plan, identifying any particular skills that need to be acquired for the proposed research to succeed. Training is a key part of the proposed fellowship, whether helping an AI expert master a scientific topic or a scientific topic expert advance their AI skills. Deep expertise in AI (or the particular Science area) is not a pre-requisite: the minimum level of AI/Science experience is that needed to credibly articulate a plan for how AI will advance Science. Fit to AI in Science Remit: a ¼ page or less outline of how your proposal fits within the AI in Science remit – please check your proposal’s fit here . Kindness Statement: a ¼ page or less outline of your view on the need for kindness among scientists. The fellows will join a cohort of fellows in AI for Science with opportunities for outreach and LMIC engagement. Mobility Statement (for applicants currently at Imperial only): Schmidt Sciences seeks to enable national and international mobility: candidates from outside Imperial are particularly encouraged to apply. Candidates that are already in Imperial are asked to supply several sentences in their mobility statement outlining, in a distinctive and compelling manner, how remaining in Imperial is the best route for their personal growth. Applicants from Imperial are required to move from their existing supervisor to entirely new mentors for their Schmidt Fellowship, to increase both their independence and their breadth of experience (existing mentors cannot become secondary mentors). Joint Fellowship We have partnered with the Institute of Cancer Research (ICR) and the CNRS. If you are applying for a joint fellowship, we require that the principal mentor be in Imperial, but a secondary supervisor must be at ICR/CNRS. Please indicate if this is a joint fellowship by ticking the appropriate ICR/CNRS tick box in the online application form. Please note that these applications will require an institutional letter of support and ICR and CNRS will have their own internal process with a deadline ahead of the closing date below. The ICR deadline will be Monday 29 th June at 9am . You must contact Becky Cook ( rebecca.cook@icr.ac.uk ) at ICR and Carl Iamov carl.ialamov@cnrs.fr and/or Louis Avigdor louis.avigdor@cnrs-dir.fr at CNRS to confirm these internal processes and deadlines. £55,144 per annum

new_jobs.title: PhD Studentship: Epilepsy Research Institute UK PhD Fellowship: Extracting added value from home EEG epilepsy diagnostics for children and young adults
new_jobs.description: The King’s College London research division of Digital Health and Applied Technology Assessment (DHATA) is inviting applications for a PhD studentship to commence on Monday 21 September 2026. One in 100 people have epilepsy, which accounts for 1% of all healthy life years lost in Europe. Its negative consequences are worsened by the diagnostic delay that occurs in up to 77% of patients, which predicts extra adverse neurodevelopment in children. This PhD project aims to improve and personalise epilepsy diagnostics for children and young adults, by applying new analytics to clinical home electroencephalography (EEG). The objectives are to leverage untapped value from ‘non-diagnostic’ EEG, by analysing i) sleep and ii) heart rate variability, and iii) stratifying data by multi-morbidity profile. The PhD will be supervised by Dr Kimberley Whitehead (DHATA, Florence Nightingale Faculty of Nursing, Midwifery & Palliative Care (NMPC)) and Dr Joel Winston (Institute of Psychiatry, Psychology & Neuroscience, IoPPN). The successful candidate will join a dynamic community of researchers at King’s who are studying epilepsy , neurodevelopment, and neurophysiology using signal processing and data science methods. In addition to project-specific training, the student will have free access to our in-person MRes Clinical Research module in Advanced Quantitative Research Methods , and a comprehensive programme of professional development courses. There is a lively post-graduate research culture at King’s, including 3-minute thesis events and an annual symposium. This award is open to Home-fee students only. The appointee will have a strong background in health data science, software engineering, neuroscience, or similar. Applicants will normally hold, or be about to complete, a Master’s degree in a relevant field (Merit or above) or have equivalent experience, e.g. working full-time as a Research Assistant for a year. Programming proficiency is essential, ideally in MATLAB and/or R, and experience of time series analysis is desirable. We are looking for somebody who will contribute to and benefit from the vibrant in-person research culture at NMPC and IoPPN. This opportunity is not suitable for those who require to work from home. The successful candidate will have excellent communication skills with both scientific and lay audiences, as this project will involve patient and public engagement. We are also seeking an appointee who is motivated, passionate about healthcare science, and able to work independently and as part of a multidisciplinary team. This project is being generously funded by the Epilepsy Research Institute ’s supporters, including those affected by epilepsy. There is an expectation that the candidate will engage with the donor. Applicants must be committed to this PhD opportunity, and preference will be given to those who have a proven ability to complete and deliver projects. Informal enquiries and visits to Dr Whitehead at her research division are encouraged ( kimberley.whitehead@kcl.ac.uk ). Candidates must apply via the King’s Apply system, for Health Studies Research MPhil/PhD (Full-time) . Quote the project title, and indicate Dr Whitehead as primary and Dr Winston as secondary supervisor. Please include: Your CV to ‘Employment History’. A 500-word statement outlining how you meet the eligibility criteria to ‘Supporting Statement’. Funding: Please choose Option 5 “I am applying for a funding award administered by King’s” and under “Award Scheme Code” enter 26KERI . Two references, with at least one being academic. We encourage applicants to send the same paperwork to Dr Whitehead at the time that they submit it to King’s Apply. For eligible students, this award will cover Home tuition fees, an annual tax-free stipend of approximately £24,643 for 3 years full-time, and a contribution to UK conference costs.

new_jobs.description: This post is full time (35 hours per week) and has funding available for up to 3 years. Relocation assistance will be provided where appropriate. The School of Computing Science are looking for a post-doctoral researcher to join a research project which brings the Active Inference perspective to the study of human computer interaction. This requires us to build/learn generative, probabilistic forward models of users and their physical and computational environments. This will involve modelling sensors, developing dynamic models for control and developing a theory for future direct manipulation interactions with AI-enhanced systems outside of language-model-based thinking. The successful candidate will be someone with the ability to develop novel theory, and a strong background in topics such as Active Inference, Model-based Reinforcement Learning, or Model-based predictive control. Ideally they should also have an interest in HCI theory. They must also have strong development skills, to enable them to lead the process of prototyping new interactive systems with sensors, build machine learning algorithms that work in-the-loop, and deliver empirical work and publications. We can offer opportunities for you to develop in your career and make a real difference in the communities around us while contributing to the world at large. The University of Edinburgh is a world-class organisation. We look for the best in the field across all disciplines and provide a working environment where academics can develop their careers and passion for their chosen subject area. We offer the full range of academic roles and have a genuine focus on our student’s performance and wellbeing. Improving diagnosis for rare genetic diseases is a critical challenge, requiring innovative approaches to extract and interpret complex biological and clinical information. As part of the Welcome Trust funded PARADIGM project , an initiative aiming to transform genomic medicine by identifying new causes of monogenic disease and developing annotated resources for the scientific community. This role will focus on advancing biomedical natural language processing (NLP) to enhance variant-phenotype mapping, gene-disease models, and clinical decision support. The successful candidate will collaborate with academics, curators, clinical scientists, clinicians, and patient groups to identify practical applications of NLP in real-world scenarios. Key responsibilities include developing novel NLP approaches to extract phenotypic information from unstructured data (e.g., literature, EHRs, clinical notes), creating domain-specific text summarisation tools, and integrating these technologies into PARADIGM’s broader research goals. The role also involves proactively disseminating work through national and international collaborations, ensuring explainable AI/ML for clinical use, and supporting students through mentorship and knowledge-sharing activities. This position offers the opportunity to contribute to cutting-edge research, work in the Biomedical Informatics Group , a multidisciplinary team in the School of Informatics, and shape the future of genomic medicine. Your skills and attributes for success: Essential Skills & Experience: PhD (or equivalent) in a relevant field (e.g., Natural Language Processing, Bioinformatics, Computational Biology, or related disciplines). Demonstrated expertise in biomedical NLP, including experience with text mining, knowledge extraction, or clinical NLP, Strong programming skills in Python and familiarity with NLP toolkits. Experience with large language models (LLMs) and their fine-tuning for domain-specific tasks. Ability to work collaboratively within interdisciplinary teams (academics, clinicians, curators, etc.) and communicate complex technical concepts clearly. Experience with scientific writing, including the ability to publish in peer-reviewed journals and present research findings at conferences. Desirable Skills & Experience: Knowledge of genetic disease biology, phenotypic data, or clinical workflows (e.g., EHRs, patient registries) Experience with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems or prompt engineering for NLP tasks. Familiarity with agentic systems or autonomous workflows for data processing and analysis. Experience with literature analysis, including the use of tools for full-text document retrieval. Familiarity with variant-phenotype mapping, gene-disease model creation, or computational phenomics. Experience with explainable AI/ML for clinical decision support or open-source software development. £41,064 to £48,822 per annum (Grade 7)

new_jobs.description: Fixed-term for 2 years Location: Wolfson Building, Parks Road, Oxford We are looking for a postdoctoral research associate position is available in the Oxford Machine Learning in Neuroimaging (OMNI) lab at Oxford’s Department of Computer Science. The post-holder will report directly to Professor Ana Namburete and will work within a multidisciplinary team of deep learning researchers, research software engineers, and obstetricians, and will collaborate with Dr João Henriques (Visual Geometry Group, VGG) and Professor Aris Papageorghiou (Nuffield Department of Women’s and Reproductive Health). The project focuses on efficient neural implicit representations and neural rendering methods for volumetric reconstruction from freehand ultrasound sweeps acquired with handheld probes. The overarching goal is to enable near-real-time 3D reconstruction at the bedside, supporting quantitative assessment of fetal brain development in low-resource settings. This project is being generously funded by the Epilepsy Research Institute ’s supporters, including those affected by epilepsy. There is an expectation that the candidate will engage with the donor. Applicants must be committed to this PhD opportunity, and preference will be given to those who have a proven ability to complete and deliver projects. Informal enquiries and visits to Dr Whitehead at her research division are encouraged ( kimberley.whitehead@kcl.ac.uk ). Candidates must apply via the King’s Apply system, for Health Studies Research MPhil/PhD (Full-time) . Quote the project title, and indicate Dr Whitehead as primary and Dr Winston as secondary supervisor. Please include: Your CV to ‘Employment History’. A 500-word statement outlining how you meet the eligibility criteria to ‘Supporting Statement’. Funding: Please choose Option 5 “I am applying for a funding award administered by King’s” and under “Award Scheme Code” enter 26KERI . Two references, with at least one being academic. We encourage applicants to send the same paperwork to Dr Whitehead at the time that they submit it to King’s Apply. For eligible students, this award will cover Home tuition fees, an annual tax-free stipend of approximately £24,643 for 3 years full-time, and a contribution to UK conference costs.
